2. Report Structure & Format.section_structure: 5/5 — all key sections present (4/4), 38 headings total

3. Content Completeness.technology_evolution: 5/5 — Section 2 clearly and chronologically covers concatenative and statistical parametric (HMM/DNN) methods, then the 2016–2019 neural shift (WaveNet, Tacotron/Tacotron2, two-stage acoustic model+vocoder), and finally 2020–2025 diversification (NAR, end-to-end, diffusion/flow, codec LMs). Evolution is traced with concrete examples and implications (speed, alignment, pipeline structure).
3. Content Completeness.architecture_analysis: 5/5 — Provides substantive technical discussion of multiple architecture families: non-autoregressive (FastSpeech 2 variance adaptor), end-to-end VAE+GAN with MAS (VITS), diffusion (Diff-TTS, Grad-TTS score matching, ProDiff distillation), flow models (Glow-TTS, Flowtron), hybrids (SimpleSpeech 2), and codec language models (VALL-E/VALL-E2, NaturalSpeech 2). Includes principles and representative works well beyond the minimum of four.
3. Content Completeness.technical_challenges: 5/5 — Section 4 discusses key challenges explicitly required: speed–quality tradeoff with specific mitigation techniques (distillation, grouped code modeling, hybrids), expressive diversity/controllability with disentanglement difficulty and representative solutions, cross-lingual/cross-speaker generalization with phonological/accent issues and cited models, and data efficiency (scaling vs low-resource). Adds robustness/safety, which strengthens completeness.
3. Content Completeness.evaluation_framework: 4/5 — Covers subjective evaluation (MOS, CMOS, SMOS, AB/ABX) with limitations and standardization mention, and objective metrics (MCD, WER, embedding cosine similarity, F0 errors, RTF) plus learned MOS predictors (UTMOS/DNSMOS) and RLHF tie-in. However, the prompt’s exemplars PESQ, STOI, and FAD are not included, and objective metric caveats/frontier comparisons are not as systematic across commonly used perceptual/objective measures.
3. Content Completeness.future_directions: 4/5 — Section 7 proposes multiple concrete future directions (RLHF for speech, Schrödinger bridges, multimodal foundation models, personalization, safety/watermarking, low-resource languages) tied to current trends. Nonetheless, it lacks a structured short/medium/long-term technical roadmap with clear milestones/tradeoffs; predictions are plausible but not organized as an actionable timeline.

4. Technical Depth.math_formulas: 1/5 — The report accurately names key methods (CVAE, MAS, score matching, diffusion, normalizing flows) but provides no explicit mathematical formulations (e.g., ELBO, diffusion forward/reverse equations, flow Jacobian likelihood) and no algorithm pseudocode or computational complexity (Big-O).
4. Technical Depth.industry_cases: 1/5 — It references a Microsoft system via Blizzard Challenge 2023 and discusses research systems (e.g., VALL-E/VITS), but does not analyze multiple real-world commercial/production deployments (Google Cloud TTS, Azure, AWS Polly, NVIDIA Riva, iFlytek, Baidu, etc.) with architecture diagrams, latency/throughput/cost, scaling, or deployment metrics.
4. Technical Depth.ethics_security: 2/5 — Mentions misuse risks, spoofing/deepfakes, watermarking/provenance tracking, and cites ASVspoof 5, but lacks concrete detection/watermarking system descriptions and quantitative security results (AUC/EER), and omits privacy compliance (GDPR/CCPA/DPIA), fairness, and actionable technical mitigations (e.g., DP epsilons, federated setups) or a governance roadmap.
4. Technical Depth.quantitative_comparison: 2/5 — Lists evaluation metrics (MOS, RTF, WER, MCD, etc.) and includes a few isolated quantitative claims (e.g., “28× real-time”), but provides no systematic tables comparing multiple models on common benchmarks, and offers limited root-cause analysis tied to measured differences.

5. Academic Standards.IEEE_format: 3/3 — standard IEEE numbered citation format detected
5. Academic Standards.terminology_consistency: 3/4 — Technical terminology (e.g., autoregressive/non-autoregressive, diffusion/flow models, neural codec language models, MOS/WER/RTF) is generally accurate and used consistently across sections. Most acronyms are expanded on first use (e.g., TTS, HMM, DNN, MOS, WER, ASR, RTF), and key concepts are described when introduced. Minor consistency issues remain: mixed naming conventions for evaluation variants (e.g., “Similarity MOS (SMOS)” is not a widely standardized label), occasional informal phrasing within technical claims (e.g., “human parity” without an operational definition), and reference notation includes an inconsistent placeholder (“[15b]”) not reflected in the reference list. No figures/tables are present, so numbering consistency cannot be assessed.
5. Academic Standards.writing_style: 4/4 — The style is consistently academic, objective, and logically structured with clear sectioning and signposting. It predominantly uses third-person, avoids colloquial expressions, and maintains a rigorous tone appropriate for a survey report. Claims are generally framed cautiously and supported by citations, with minimal rhetorical flourish.

6. Data Analysis & Visualization.image_content_eval: 2/5 — Vision LLM unavailable, giving conservative score

eval_report.comment: Good: Report is mostly complete, main content is well-covered, but some dimensions have room for improvement.
